1:
The chapter opens on a sudden apostasy, and the witnesses read it as the classic tragedy of prosperity. The reformed John Gill sets down what was forsaken: for three years the tribes of Judah and Benjamin had been firmly attached to Rehoboam, great numbers from the other tribes had come over to him, and all had gone peaceably and prosperously so long as king and people abode by the pure worship of God. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the moment precisely - the defection came after the three faithful years noted at 2 Chronicles 11:17 - and lays out the whole arc of the chapter to come: forsaking the Lord, Rehoboam and his subjects are given into Shishak's hand, are remonstrated with by Shemaiah, humble themselves, and so are spared, though stripped of their treasures.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the bare notice into a searching moral. Judah had every advantage - the temple, the royal city, the house of David and the house of Aaron - and might have done very well had they kept to their duty; but 'he forsook the law of the Lord, and so in effect forsook God, and all Israel with him.' Henry finds the cause in the very security of the throne: as long as Rehoboam thought his crown tottered he kept close to his duty, that he might make God his friend; but when he found it stood firm he reckoned he had no more occasion for religion. 'Thus the prosperity of fools destroys them,' he writes; Jeshurun waxed fat and kicked, the ancient warning of Deuteronomy 32:15. When men prosper and fear no trouble, they are ready to say to God, Depart from us.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott secures the wording and the theology together. The opening is impersonal - better 'when Rehoboam's kingdom had been established' - and the verb rendered 'strengthened himself' is a rare infinitive found besides only at 2 Chronicles 26:16 and Daniel 11:2. To 'forsake the law of the Lord' means, he explains, to lapse into idolatry, the offence described more precisely at 1 Kings 14:22; and 'all Israel' is the chronicler's way of regarding the southern kingdom as the true Israel. The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Clarke fix the happy years now ended and the judgment now begun; Ellicott sets the philology and the sin; and Henry draws the abiding lesson, that a strength which loosens a man's hold upon God is no strength at all but the first stage of his ruin. It was when Rehoboam had established the kingdom and thought himself safe that he cast off the God who had established it.
2:
The blow falls in the fifth year, and the witnesses are careful to align the calendar of sin and punishment. The reformed John Gill notes the sequence plainly: the apostasy of king and people began in the fourth year, and in the year following came the stroke that punished it. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in a phrase - the invasion followed 'presently after the apostasy,' which was in the fourth year. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the whole order out: for three years Rehoboam and his people continued faithful, as 2 Chronicles 11:17 records; in the fourth they fell away; and in the fifth their apostasy was punished.

On the invader the witnesses turn historian. Ellicott identifies Shishak with the Sesonchis of Manetho and the sh-sh-nk of the hieroglyphs, first king of the twenty-second dynasty, whose Semitic name and those of his successors explain the close commercial dealings of Solomon's day; he dates the campaign to about 949 B.C., undertaken at the instigation of Jeroboam, and points to the southern wall of the temple at Karnak where the captured Palestinian towns are enumerated. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the monument: Shishak celebrated his victory in a relief at Karnak, and the names of the captured cities, recorded there on shields and now in good part deciphered, completely confirm the biblical account. This invasion, they add, is briefly told also at 1 Kings 14:25.

The deepest note is Ellicott's on the clause the chronicler adds and Kings omits - 'because they had transgressed.' The verse coincides with 1 Kings 14:25 almost word for word, but this closing clause is the chronicler's own parenthetic explanation of the event, and it expresses, Ellicott says, his whole philosophy of Israel's history in a single word. It is not meant, he cautions, that Shishak had any consciousness of the providential ground of his march; the Egyptian came for plunder, but the God of Judah used him for correction. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke touches the same nerve from the Targum, which reads that Judah had transgressed 'against the Word of the Lord.' The witnesses do not contend. Gill, Wesley, and Ellicott fix the year and its cause; Keil and Ellicott anchor the invader in the monuments of Egypt; and all agree that the naming of the transgression is the point of the verse - that a foreign army on the roads of Judah was, to the eye of faith, the visible sentence of a broken law.
3:
The sheer scale of the invading host draws the witnesses to numbers and nations alike. The reformed John Gill notes that the footmen who came 'without number' were reckoned by Josephus at four hundred thousand. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the twelve hundred chariots and sixty thousand horsemen as figures founded only on a rough estimate, and identify the peoples who swelled the ranks - the Lubim or Libyans, the Sukkiim whom the Septuagint and Vulgate render Troglodytes, and the Ethiopians or Cushites. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, observes that the short account in Kings says nothing of the host's numbers or make-up, and eyes the totals with some suspicion, remarking that the cavalry are exactly fifty times the chariots; the Libyans and Cushites, he notes, appear together as auxiliaries of Egypt in Nahum 3:9 and again at 2 Chronicles 16:8.

Over the obscure Sukkiim the witnesses gently divide. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes them the Troglodytes, a people on the Egyptian coast of the Red Sea, and works the sense from the Hebrew root sachah, 'to cover,' so that they were the people who dwelt covered under the earth - a derivation he supports from Hesychius and from Pliny's report that 'the Troglodytes dig themselves caves, and these serve them for houses.' His fellow wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the same name differently: they were 'a people living in tents, as the word signifies,' dwelling not far from Egypt in both Africa and Arabia. Ellicott confesses the Sukkiyans are simply unknown, though he too follows the Septuagint and Vulgate toward the cave-dwelling Troglodytes, comparing the Hebrew for 'his lair' in Psalms 10:9. So a small question stands: were these auxiliaries cave-men or tent-men, the ambiguity lying in a root that can mean either a covering booth or a covered hole.

On the Ethiopians the reading is easier. Clarke takes the Cushim for a people bordering the Red Sea but sprung from a Cush south of Egypt; Wesley leaves them 'either those beyond Egypt, or the Arabians.' What the witnesses will not let the reader miss is the theological weight of the muster: a vast coalition of Egypt and Africa, chariots and cavalry and countless foot, gathered against a little kingdom of two tribes. The numbers, whether pressed or doubted, tell of an overwhelming force - the rod God had lifted against Judah for its apostasy grown, in the witnesses' telling, to the length of a continent, so that the smallness of Judah and the vastness of the host together make the coming deliverance pure mercy.
4:
Shishak's advance sweeps up the fortresses of Judah and halts only at the capital, and the witnesses read the loss of the fenced cities as the collapse of a false security. The reformed John Gill notes that these were the cities Rehoboam had lately built and garrisoned, placing his sons in them, and that Shishak took them 'without any opposition' - the garrisons the king had so carefully manned melting away before the Egyptian at the first approach. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the irony: these were the very cities Rehoboam had fortified as bulwarks against Egypt, the whole line of defence catalogued at 2 Chronicles 11:5-12, now swallowed one after another. He turns to the Egyptian monument for confirmation - fourteen of the city-names have crumbled from the Karnak inscription, but Socho, Adoraim, and Ajalon can still be read there, silent stones bearing witness to the campaign.

Ellicott presses the historical point against the critics. The clause 'came to Jerusalem' he takes strictly - Shishak came 'so far as to' Jerusalem, laying his march against the capital as Sennacherib would later do in Isaiah 36:1-2. This verse, he observes, is not found in Kings; and even Thenius (as reported by Ellicott) grants that the chronicler has here made use of 'really historical notices' - a concession Ellicott thinks self-evident, for the Karnak reliefs corroborate the very cities the chronicler names. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add only the plain sequence: after the capture of the fenced cities of Judah, the king of Egypt marched against Jerusalem.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement makes the theological point for them. Rehoboam had trusted his walls; he had spent the peace of his early reign fortifying a ring of strongholds against exactly this danger, and every one of them fell at a touch. The cities that were to have been the kingdom's salvation proved no defence at all once God had withdrawn his protection, so that the enemy stood at the gate of Jerusalem itself. It is the lesson the chapter keeps pressing in another key: that walls and garrisons cannot hold when the Lord who alone secures a people has been forsaken, and that the fortresses of a disobedient king are but the first spoil of his punishment. Rehoboam had ringed his kingdom with strongholds precisely because he feared an invasion from the south, and the whole labour proved worthless the moment the God who alone gives victory had withdrawn. Where Gill marks the ease of the conquest and Ellicott the confirmation of the record, both leave Judah stripped of its outer defences and thrown back, undefended, upon the mercy it had forfeited.
5:
With the enemy at the walls a prophet appears, and the witnesses gather around the terrible symmetry of his message. The reformed John Gill identifies Shemaiah as the man of God who had spoken before, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry recalls that this is the same prophet who had once brought Judah God's injunction not to fight against the ten tribes, at 2 Chronicles 11:2. Now, Henry says, God by his word explains the rod: the princes had gathered at Jerusalem, likely in a council of war to scrutinize where their policy had failed, and Shemaiah tells them plainly that Shishak prevailed not because they had been impolitic in managing their affairs but because they had forsaken God. 'God never leaves any till they first leave him.'

The grammatical-historical witnesses fasten on the exact reciprocity of the oracle. Charles Ellicott hears the emphasis fall on the pronoun: 'Ye have forsaken me, and I also have forsaken you, into the hand of Shishak.' The section recording Shemaiah's mission and its results, he notes, is peculiar to the chronicle, absent from Kings; the princes, repulsed by the Egyptian arms, had fallen back upon Jerusalem to defend the capital, and it was there, with the invading host before the city, that the prophet addressed them. The phrase 'to leave into the hand' of a foe recurs at Nehemiah 9:28, and the words here, Ellicott judges, amount to a menace of utter destruction, like the bare sentence of doom in Jonah 3:4. The same law of dealing is set down as a naked principle at 2 Chronicles 15:2 - 'if ye forsake him, he will forsake you' - so that Shemaiah does no more than apply to Judah's crisis a settled rule of God's government. Keil and Delitzsch read the same measure-for-measure: because they had forsaken the Lord, he had forsaken them and given them over into the power of Shishak.

The witnesses do not divide; they let the justice of the thing stand bare. The sentence is not arbitrary but answering - God withdraws exactly as he was abandoned, and hands the kingdom over to the very power it had feared. Yet even here, Henry notes, mercy is at work, for God 'explains the rod' rather than striking in silence; he sends a word before he sends the worst, so that the stroke may be understood and the sinner recalled. The prophet's errand, harsh as it sounds, is the first motion of grace: a God who meant only ruin would not have troubled to tell them why they suffered. The terror of 'I also have forsaken you' is set down precisely so that the humbling of the next verse may follow.
6:
The word does its work, and king and princes bow. The reformed John Gill reads the humbling as both inward and outward - by words, acknowledging their sins and the justice of God, and by deeds, perhaps putting on sackcloth and betaking themselves to fasting and prayer, as was usual on such occasions. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in their confession, 'The Lord is righteous,' the true note of penitence: they acknowledged the sin and accepted the punishment, saying in effect, 'We have none to blame but ourselves; let God be clear when he judgeth.' Thus it becomes us, Henry urges, under the rebukes of Providence to justify God and judge ourselves; even kings and princes must either bend or break before God, either be humbled or be ruined.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks how much hung on that bowing. This humbling is not mentioned in the parallel at 1 Kings 14:25-29, yet it was, he insists, 'the sole reason why Jerusalem was not at this time totally destroyed, and the house of David entirely cut off'; for they were wholly incapable of defending themselves against so innumerable a host. The single act of self-abasement, invisible to the Egyptian, was the hinge on which the survival of David's line turned.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott secures the words. The 'princes of Israel' here are the princes of Judah of the previous verse, the southern kingdom being counted the true Israel; 'humbled themselves' is literally 'bowed,' the same self-abasement promised its reward at 2 Chronicles 7:14. He sets the whole scene beside the repentance of Nineveh at Jonah 3:5-6, where king and people alike bowed under a prophet's word of doom; and their cry 'The Lord is righteous' he ranges with the confession wrung even from Pharaoh in Exodus 9:27 and the penitent's word in Ezra 9:15. The witnesses do not divide. Gill describes the penitence, Henry commends it, Clarke measures its saving weight, and Ellicott traces its language back through Scripture. Together they make the verse the chapter's turning-point: a kingdom that had forsaken its God, cornered and defenceless, confesses his justice, and in that confession - not in any wall or army - finds the ground on which mercy can stand. There is a bitter aptness, too, in the witnesses' cross-references: the very words 'The Lord is righteous' had once been forced from Pharaoh under the plagues, and are now taken up freely by the sons of the people Pharaoh had oppressed, so that Judah in her humbling speaks the language of the Egyptian she is about to serve. The self-condemning word is the first true thing Judah has said since it fell away, and it is enough.
7:
God sees the bowed heart and answers, and the witnesses weigh both the mercy granted and the exact measure of it. The reformed John Gill observes at once that the humiliation was 'but externally' true, and that the Lord takes notice even of an outward humbling, as he did of Ahab's in 1 Kings 21:29 - a mercy shown to repentance that was more show than substance. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears the sentence 'My wrath shall not be poured out upon Jerusalem' as the destroying angel forbidden to strike: not at this time, not by this hand, not utterly to destroy. 'Those that acknowledge God righteous in afflicting them shall find him gracious,' he writes; so ready is the God of mercy to take the first occasion to show mercy.

On the phrase rendered 'some deliverance' the grammatical-historical witnesses part a little from the older reading. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott would render it not 'some deliverance' but 'I will give them a few for a remnant,' comparing the promise not to destroy 'altogether' in v.12 and the plea for a remnant at Ezra 9:13; the word he reads as 'few,' its pointing peculiar to this passage, the same 'little' remnant that the prophet owns in Isaiah 1:9 as all that God's mercy had left of a stricken people. Keil and Delitzsch, with the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, take the same word as temporal - 'according to a little,' that is, in a short time; Wesley paraphrases, 'I will give some stop to the course of my wrath, which was ready to be poured forth upon them to their utter destruction.' So the witnesses divide over whether God promised to spare a small remnant of the people or to grant a brief respite of deliverance - a genuine difference in the Hebrew that the schools resolve in opposite directions.

On the weight of 'my wrath shall not be poured out' they are one. Ellicott and Keil agree that the pouring out of anger is the standing phrase for a judgment of extermination, its force felt again at 2 Chronicles 34:25; and Ellicott adds the sombre reservation that the destruction thus withheld from Shishak's hand was only deferred, kept back for the hand of Nebuchadnezzar in a later age. The mercy, then, is real but measured: whether read as a remnant or a respite, it stays the sword without breaking it, and the God who will not pour out his wrath today does not promise never to pour it out at all. Judah is spared, not acquitted.
8:
The reprieve comes with a discipline attached - 'they shall be his servants' - and the witnesses find in that servitude a lesson God means his people to learn by the hard road of experience. The reformed John Gill takes it plainly of their becoming tributaries to the king of Egypt. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the divine intention: they shall be preserved, yet made to serve their enemies, 'that they may see the difference between the service of God and that of man'; while they were pious they found the service of the Lord to be perfect freedom, and when they forsook him they found its fruit to be perfect bondage. 'A sinful life,' he says, 'is both expensive and painful.' His fellow wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same - that they may 'experimentally know the difference between my yoke and the yoke of a foreign and idolatrous prince.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the clause 'for they shall become servants to him,' and only for a while; the purpose is that they may learn by experience the difference between the easy yoke of their God and the heavy burden of the foreign tyranny they had entailed upon themselves by deserting him. The phrase 'kingdoms of the countries,' he notes, is the chronicler's own idiom, drawn from 1 Chronicles 29:30. Keil and Delitzsch read it identically: they shall be his servants for a short time, that they may come to know by experience the contrast between serving the Lord and serving the powers of the earth.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the lesson its sharpest edge. The people, he supposes, had grumbled that God's service was a yoke too heavy, and so forsook the law; God answers by letting the neighbouring princes rule them awhile, 'since they are not willing that I should rule them.' They might have served God with joyfulness and gladness of heart and would not; let them then serve their enemies in hunger and thirst, as Deuteronomy 28:47-48 had warned, till they think of returning to their first Master, when it was better with them, after the pattern of Hosea 2:7. The witnesses converge on one great antithesis, which Henry states as a maxim and the others feel as a fact: 'the service of virtue is perfect liberty; the service of lust is perfect slavery.' Judah is handed over to Egypt not to be destroyed but to be taught, by the galling weight of a stranger's yoke, how light the yoke of God had been.
9:
The account of the war resumes, and Shishak carries off the wealth of Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the narrative is taken up again by the repetition of Shishak's march against the city; he plundered the treasures of the temple and the palace, and had therefore taken Jerusalem itself. The golden shields that Solomon had made and hung in the house of the forest of Lebanon - the palace named at 2 Chronicles 9:16 - he bore away, so that Rehoboam must replace them with brass, the whole exchange already told at 1 Kings 14:26-28. Charles Ellicott confirms that the verse resumes the story after the parenthesis on Shemaiah, and that the taking of the temple treasures stands word for word as in 1 Kings 14:26.

The reformed John Gill adds the aftermath from the older account and the versions: having taken the city and spoiled its riches, Shishak settled a yearly tax upon the inhabitants of the land, and the emphatic 'he took all' means all he could lay his hands on. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marvels at the magnitude of the plunder - 'such a booty as never had before, nor has since, come into the hand of man' - and explains the golden shields as the mark of the king's body-guard, the very magnificence that Alexander would afterwards imitate when he formed his Argyraspides, or Silver-Shields, adorned with the spoils taken from Darius.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the emptied treasuries as a parable of the reign. Shishak plundered both the temple and the exchequer, whose stores Solomon had left so full; David and Solomon, who walked in the way of God, had filled the treasuries, the one by war and the other by merchandise, but Rehoboam, who forsook the law of God, emptied them. 'This was what he came for,' Henry says of the Egyptian, and the point is not lost: the gold that faithful kings had gathered was carried into Egypt by the folly of an unfaithful one. The witnesses do not divide. Keil and Ellicott fix the resumption of the narrative and the fate of the golden shields; Gill and Clarke measure the greatness of the spoil and the tribute that followed; and Henry draws the moral that a kingdom's wealth is held only on the tenure of its obedience, and that what devotion lays up, apostasy pours out into the lap of the enemy. The gold that had made Jerusalem the wonder of the nations in Solomon's day is gone in a single campaign, and the loss is not merely material: the shields of the temple and the treasures of the house of the Lord, dedicated things, pass into a heathen hand because the king who should have guarded them had first let go his hold on the God whose house it was.
10:
For the plundered gold Rehoboam substitutes brass, and the witnesses attend to the poverty hidden in the ceremony. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the plain fact: in place of the shields Shishak had carried off, King Rehoboam made shields of brass and committed them to the hands of the chief of the guard that kept the entrance of the king's house. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, sends the reader to the parallel at 1 Kings 14:27, with which the verse coincides, and sharpens the title of the officers who received them: 'chief of the guard' is literally 'captains of the runners,' the couriers who ran before the king and stood at his gate.

The substitution itself is the thing the witnesses will not let pass as a mere note of inventory. Solomon's shields had been of beaten gold, the ornament of the house of the forest of Lebanon and the emblem of the royal state; Rehoboam cannot restore what has been lost, and so replaces gold with brass - a baser metal shaped to the same form, kept and paraded as though nothing had changed. The grammatical-historical care for the words only underlines the pathos: the shields are still made, still committed to the captains of the runners, still guarded at the entrance of the king's house, but the glory is counterfeit, brass burnished to look like the gold it can never be.

The custody is significant too. The brazen shields are not left in the treasury but put into the keeping of the royal guard at the palace gate, ready for the ceremonial use the next verse will describe. Rehoboam, stripped of his father's treasure, is careful at least to maintain the appearance of a court, to keep up the show of a kingdom that had lately been the richest in the world. The witnesses, both of the grammatical-historical school, do not divide; they simply fix what was made and who kept it, and leave the reader to feel the difference between the reign that gathered gold and the reign that must make do with brass. It is the whole judgment of the chapter in miniature: a king who forsook the law of the Lord and found, when the enemy had gone, that he could recover his strongholds and his ceremonies but not the substance of the glory his sin had given away. What the fathers won in gold the son keeps in brass, and the exchange is a standing monument, hung at his own gate, of what apostasy costs.
11:
The brazen shields have their appointed office, and the witnesses describe the small recurring ritual of the diminished court. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening as a frequentative - 'and as often as' the king went into the house of the Lord - and pictures the scene: the runners came and bore the shields before him, and then, when the royal procession was over, carried them back and restored them to the guard-chamber of the runners. He points to the parallel at 1 Kings 14:28, where 'the runners used to bear them,' and reminds the reader that Solomon's original shields of gold had been kept in the house of the forest of Lebanon, named at 2 Chronicles 9:16. Keil and Delitzsch record the same coming and going: when the king entered the house of the Lord the guard fetched the shields, and afterward brought them again into the guard-chamber.

What the witnesses establish by their care for the wording is the shape of a habit - a thing done again and again, whenever the king went up to worship. The shields were brought out for the procession to the temple, borne in state before the king as the golden ones had once been borne before Solomon, and then quietly locked away again until the next occasion. The frequentative that Ellicott recovers turns a single act into a settled custom of the reign: this was how Rehoboam went up to the house of God, guarded by runners bearing brass.

The pathos of it lies in the very regularity. Every time the king made his solemn way to the temple, the shields that flashed before him were the emblem of a loss, a recurring reminder that the gold was in Egypt and only the brass remained. The house of the forest of Lebanon, where Solomon's shields had hung in rows, now held them no more; the guard-chamber of the runners held their base replacements instead. The witnesses do not divide, and both being of the grammatical-historical school, they press no moral upon the scene; they fix the ritual and its recurrence and leave it there. Yet the plain description they secure carries its own quiet sermon. A king may keep up every form of his father's magnificence - the procession to worship, the borne shields, the attending guard - and yet, having forsaken the law of the Lord, carry brass where his father carried gold, going up to God's house with the marks of his own chastisement gleaming in his train.
12:
The chronicler gathers the meaning of the whole episode into a single verdict - 'when he humbled himself, the wrath of the Lord turned from him' - and the witnesses read it as the theological heart of the chapter. The reformed John Gill notes honestly that Rehoboam 'broke his heart,' as the Targum has it, and was of a contrite spirit only seemingly, for the humbling was merely external; yet God regarded it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks that this remark, in perfect accord with the chronicler's conception of what Shishak's invasion really meant, is wanting in Kings; it is his own comment, fulfilling the promise given through Shemaiah at v.7. The pledge not to destroy him 'altogether' Ellicott renders 'unto consumption,' a rare phrase found besides only at Ezekiel 13:13, and takes it of a general destruction of the country now averted. Keil and Delitzsch read the didactic clause the same way - because Rehoboam humbled himself, the anger of the Lord turned from him, and it was 'not to extermination utterly.'

Over the last clause - 'and also in Judah things went well' - the witnesses draw slightly apart. Ellicott renders it 'moreover in Judah there were good things,' and takes it of a surviving faithfulness: the fact that some loyalty to Jehovah was still to be found in Judah is alleged as a further reason why the Lord spared the land, the same phrase recurring in that moral sense at 2 Chronicles 19:3. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads 'good things' both ways, of piety and of prosperity: there were good ministers, good people, good families, a remnant among whom good things were found even in a degenerate time, and with their repentance the posture of affairs altered so that things went better than before. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley leans wholly to the recovery - 'they began to recruit themselves, and regain some degree of their former prosperity.'

So a mild question runs beneath the agreement: were the 'good things in Judah' a moral remnant of faithfulness that moved God to spare, as Ellicott urges, or the returning prosperity of a chastened people, as Wesley takes it, or both together, as Henry allows? On the main point the witnesses are one. The turning of God's wrath is bound fast to the humbling of the king; the mercy is not earned by any excellence but drawn out by the bowing of a proud heart, however imperfect. Gill's candour that the contrition was 'merely external' only deepens the wonder - that God should stay an exterminating judgment for a repentance he himself knew to be shallow, and should find, even in a half-hearted Judah, 'good things' enough to spare.
13:
The chronicler closes the reign with the formulas of the royal annals, and the witnesses divide their attention between the king's late recovery and a knot in his recorded age. The reformed John Gill notes that after Shishak departed Rehoboam 'strengthened himself in Jerusalem,' fortifying the city yet more for his own defence. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same word as a regaining of strength after the crushing blow of the invasion: he reigned on for twelve years more, seventeen in all, in the city the Lord had chosen out of all the tribes to put his name there; the closing notice of his age and of his Ammonite mother Naamah stands word for word as at 1 Kings 14:21. Keil and Delitzsch likewise take the verb of Rehoboam's strengthening himself after his humiliation at Shishak's hands.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the geography into irony. Rehoboam's fenced cities had not answered his expectation - they had fallen at a touch - so now he made it his business to fortify Jerusalem, and there he reigned, in the holy city, near the temple but far from God. That his royal seat stood where the Lord had chosen to put his name was his honour and privilege, yet only an aggravation of his impiety. Frequent skirmishes with Jeroboam's men amounted to continual wars, Henry notes, yet Rehoboam held his own, and it seems he did not in his later years so grossly forsake the law as he had done at first.

Over the king's age the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke raises a spirited dissent. The text makes Rehoboam 'one and forty years old' at his accession, but Clarke, following Houbigant, brings 'many and forcible arguments' that the number must be a mistake for sixteen. Rehoboam was plainly young when he came to the throne, for he took counsel of the young men brought up with him at 2 Chronicles 10:8; Abijah's speech at 2 Chronicles 13:7 calls him then 'tender-hearted' and inexperienced, which no man above forty could be reckoned; and if forty-one were right, Rehoboam would have been born before his father Solomon began his forty-year reign. So the witnesses stand at odds over a figure: the received 'forty-one,' which Ellicott and the others transmit unchallenged from Kings, against Houbigant's 'sixteen,' which Clarke defends as truer to the young and pliable king the history everywhere shows. On the substance of the reign, however, they agree - a king strengthened again in his city, reigning out his seventeen years, honoured with the holy place and dishonoured by his heart.
14:
The epitaph of Rehoboam's character is written in a single clause - 'he did evil, because he prepared not his heart to seek the Lord' - and the witnesses gather to weigh where exactly the fault lay. The reformed John Gill takes 'he did evil' of a relapse into idolatry, his humiliation having proved neither hearty nor sincere. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the verb as 'settled not': though he humbled himself for a season, he quickly relapsed into sin, because his heart was not right with God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the Syriac adds 'before the Lord,' and that the nature of the evil is explained at once - 'for he directed not his heart to seek Jehovah'; the phrase 'to prepare the heart to seek the Lord' recurs at 2 Chronicles 19:3, 2 Chronicles 30:19, and Ezra 7:10, and the estimate seems to refer chiefly to the early years that ended in Shishak's invasion. Keil and Delitzsch read it plainly: he did evil because he prepared not his heart to seek the Lord.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry unfolds the clause into a whole anatomy of a divided soul. Rehoboam was never rightly fixed in his religion; he never quite cast off God, and yet in this he did evil, that he engaged not his heart to seek the Lord. Henry lays the fault at two doors. He did not serve the Lord because he did not seek the Lord - he did not pray, as Solomon prayed, for wisdom and grace, nor consult the word of God as his oracle; 'if we prayed better, we should be every way better.' And he made nothing of his religion because he did not set his heart to it, never minding it with any closeness of application nor ever coming up to a steady resolution. What little goodness he had was transient, passing away like the morning cloud of Hosea 6:4; he did evil because he was never determined for good, and 'those are easily drawn by Satan to any evil who are wavering and inconstant in that which is good.'

The witnesses do not divide; they converge on a single diagnosis. It was not that Rehoboam was a settled idolater or an open enemy of God - Henry grants he never wholly cast God off - but that his heart was unprepared, unfixed, unresolved. Ellicott's philology, Gill's and Wesley's note of the hollow repentance, Keil's bare restatement, and Henry's searching portrait all fasten on the same want: a will that would not gather itself to seek God. The tragedy of the reign, in their common reading, is not a great wickedness but a fatal irresolution - a man undone less by what he chose than by his never truly choosing at all.
15:
The closing notice sends the reader to the prophetic records and remarks the standing hostility of the two kingdoms. The reformed John Gill passes the verse quickly, referring to the fuller account. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the sense of 'concerning genealogies' from the Targum, which reads that the acts were written 'in the book of the genealogy of the family of David.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes 'the book' as a history and 'concerning genealogies' as 'for registration,' and observes that the important particulars this chronicle preserves which Kings omits - the fortification of the southern cities, the migration of the priests, Rehoboam's private family relations - were probably drawn by the chronicler from just these authorities, the records of Shemaiah the prophet and of Iddo the seer. Keil and Delitzsch note that the acts of Rehoboam 'first and last' were set down in these prophetic books, and refer the reader to the introduction for the authorities.

The last clause - 'there were wars between Rehoboam and Jeroboam continually' - raises a difficulty the witnesses take pains to resolve. Reuss (as reported by Ellicott) had charged the notice with contradicting 2 Chronicles 11:4, where the prophet forbade Judah to go up and fight against Israel. Ellicott answers that there is no contradiction: what Shemaiah forbade was a particular attempt to recover the northern kingdom by force of arms, whereas the permanent attitude of the rival kings could hardly be other than hostile - especially as Jeroboam appears to have instigated the very Egyptian invasion that fills this chapter - and this standing enmity must often have broken out into active injuries. Keil and Delitzsch reason exactly so: the 'wars' are a state of hostility all the days of the two kings, for no pitched battle between them is anywhere narrated after Rehoboam abandoned, at the prophet's word, his proposed campaign at the start of the reign.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Clarke gloss the sources, Ellicott and Keil defend the record against the charge of self-contradiction and trace the chronicler's peculiar matter to the prophetic histories he cites. Between them they leave the reign documented and its long border-feud explained: not open war, forbidden by God at the outset, but an unbroken hostility between the sundered houses of Israel, the bitter and permanent fruit of the division that had cracked the kingdom in two. Even the register of a king's acts, in their reading, carries the chapter's theme - a people at odds with its brother because it had first been at odds with its God.
16:
The reign ends with the settled formula of the royal dead - 'Rehoboam slept with his fathers, and was buried in the city of David; and Abijah his son reigned in his stead' - and the witnesses, few and brief here, catch what dignity and what question the words carry. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply record the notice: Rehoboam laid down with his fathers, was buried in the city of David, and his son succeeded him. Charles Ellicott observes that the verse is abridged from 1 Kings 14:31, and turns to the son's name. He is called Abijah here and at 2 Chronicles 11:22, whereas Kings spells it Abijam; the difference, Ellicott judges, is probably due to an accident of transcription rather than any real variation of the name.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke touches the same point, noting the many varieties in this king's name and referring the reader to his fuller treatment at 1 Kings 14:31. The small textual question - Abijah or Abijam - is all the difficulty the verse presents; the rest is the plain, grave language of succession, one king laid in his grave and his son set upon the throne.

Yet the brief obituary is not without weight when set in the chapter's frame. Rehoboam is buried 'in the city of David,' among the kings of Judah, in the royal tombs of the house from which the Messiah would come - an honour the chapter has done nothing to earn him, for he was the king who forsook the law of the Lord and prepared not his heart to seek him. He goes to his fathers with the outward dignity of David's city, though his reign was one of apostasy, invasion, and plundered treasure; and the line runs quietly on, from an unfaithful father to a son who will walk, for the most part, in the same unsteady way. The witnesses do not divide. Keil states the fact, Ellicott and Clarke settle the name, and the formula does its accustomed work - closing a reign that began in strength and folly and ends here in the tombs of David, the covenant with that house still standing over a king who scarcely honoured it. There is a quiet contrast in that resting-place worth the marking: where the kings of the northern calf-kingdom would be laid in Samaria, a capital of their own devising, Rehoboam is gathered to the city of David, the place the Lord had chosen to put his name, so that even in death the son of Solomon is held within the promise his fathers received. That the son reigns 'in his stead' is itself the quiet mercy of the chapter: the house of David, so nearly cut off by Shishak and so little deserving to continue, continues; and the line that will run down to the Messiah is not broken by the failure of the man who now sleeps with his fathers.
